content.problemStatement:
Chef purchased a new herbicide, and now wants to test its effectiveness.

Initially, there are no weeds in Chef's garden.

For each i=1,2,3,…,Ni = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, Ni=1,2,3,…,N, **exactly one** new weed will pop up in the garden at the start of day AiA_iAi​.

It is known that A1<A2<…<ANA_1 \lt A_2 \lt \ldots \lt A_NA1​<A2​<…<AN​.

Chef can spray the herbicide at most once per day.

Any weed that receives the spray KKK or more times in total will die.

Chef would like to know: is it possible that after MMM days, all the weeds are gone?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers NNN, MMM, and KKK — the number of weeds, the total number of days, and the number of sprays needed to kill a weed, respectively.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​: the days on which new weeds show up.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `Yes` if all the weeds are gone after MMM days, and `No` otherwise.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `Yes`, `YES`, `yes`, and `YeS` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains three integers N, M and K – the number of weeds, the total number of days and the number of sprays required to kill a weed. The second line contains N distinct integers A1, A2, …, AN in strictly increasing order – the days on which new weeds appear.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if Chef can make all weeds disappear after M days, otherwise output "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: K=1, any weed needs only one spray. Chef can spray on each day a weed appears. Test case 2: K=2, the last weed appears on day 10, but only one day (day 10) remains, insufficient for two sprays. Test case 3: K=5, days 4‑10 give 7 possible spray days, enough for 5 sprays.
Only one weed appears on day 1, K=1, and there is exactly one day to spray.
The last weed appears on day 12. Days 12‑15 provide four spray days, satisfying K=4.

solution.approach: A spray performed on a day affects every weed that is already present. Therefore a weed that appears on day Ai will receive a spray on each day that Chef decides to spray after (and including) Ai. To kill all weeds each of them must receive at least K sprays, which means there must be at least K spray days that are not earlier than the latest appearing weed. Consequently the condition reduces to: the last weed appears on day A_N, so we need K days in the interval [A_N, M]. This is possible iff A_N + K - 1 ≤ M. The algorithm simply checks this inequality for each test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case to read the array, O(1) extra work. Overall O(total N) across all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional besides the input array.
